product_overview: A coin change application that helps users find the minimum number of coins needed to form a target amount using different algorithms. It also provides a comparative analysis of greedy, dynamic programming, and backtracking approaches through benchmark charts and summary results.
core_goals: Enter coin denominations and a target amount to solve the coin change problem
Compare greedy, dynamic programming, and backtracking solutions for the same input
View the coins used, number of coins, optimality, and execution time for each algorithm
Run performance analysis across increasing amounts and review benchmark charts and summary metrics

features.name: Solve Coin Change (HTML)
features.description: Lets users submit coin denominations and an amount, choose one or more algorithms, and view the computed results in a browser page.
user_flows: Navigate to / -> Enter coin denominations as a comma-separated list and enter a target amount -> Select one or more algorithms -> Submit the form -> See the results page with coins used, coin count, optimality, and execution time for each selected algorithm
Navigate to / -> Enter invalid coin denominations or leave the algorithm selection empty -> Submit the form -> See an error message prompting for valid input or algorithm selection

features.name: Solve Coin Change (REST API)
features.description: Provides a JSON endpoint for solving the coin change problem programmatically with one or more algorithms.
user_flows: Send a GET request to /api/solve with coin denominations, target amount, and optional algorithm choices -> Receive a JSON response containing the computed results for each requested algorithm

features.name: Greedy Algorithm
features.description: Solves coin change by repeatedly choosing the largest possible coin first, then reports whether the result is optimal.
user_flows: Send a GET request to /api/solve with the greedy algorithm included -> Receive the greedy result with the selected coins, total coin count, and optimality status

features.name: Dynamic Programming Algorithm
features.description: Solves coin change using a bottom-up dynamic programming table to produce the optimal coin combination.
user_flows: Send a GET request to /api/solve with the dp algorithm included -> Receive the optimal coin combination along with the number of coins and supporting result data

features.name: Backtracking Algorithm
features.description: Explores coin combinations recursively to find an optimal solution, with support limited to smaller amounts.
user_flows: Send a GET request to /api/solve with the backtracking algorithm included and an amount of 60 or less -> Receive the backtracking result with the selected coins and coin count
Send a GET request to /api/solve with the backtracking algorithm included and an amount greater than 60 -> See an error response indicating the amount is too large for backtracking

features.name: Performance Analysis
features.description: Benchmarks the three algorithms across increasing amounts and displays comparative charts and summary information.
user_flows: Navigate to /analysis -> Enter coin denominations and a maximum amount -> Submit the form -> See benchmark charts for execution time, coins used, memory usage, and greedy optimality gap, plus a summary table
Navigate to /analysis -> Enter valid analysis parameters -> Submit the form -> Review comparative performance results across the tested range

routes.description: Home page with HTML form to configure coins and amount
POST endpoint that runs selected algorithms and renders results page
GET/POST endpoint for comparative performance analysis with benchmark charts
REST JSON endpoint to solve coin change and return structured results

features.name: Solve Coin Change (HTML)
features.description: Submit coin denominations and target amount, run one or more algorithms, view results in HTML
user_interactions: Submit coins as comma-separated string and integer amount via POST form
Select one or more algorithms (greedy, dp, backtracking)
View per-algorithm result including coins used, count, optimality, and execution time
See error message if no algorithm is selected or coins are invalid

features.name: Solve Coin Change (REST API)
features.description: JSON REST endpoint to solve coin change with one or more algorithms
user_interactions: Send GET request with query params coins, amount, and optional algorithms
Receive JSON response with per-algorithm results

features.name: Greedy Algorithm
features.description: Coin change using greedy approach (largest coin first); always verified against DP for optimality
user_interactions: Request greedy algorithm by including "greedy" in algorithms param

features.name: Dynamic Programming Algorithm
features.description: Optimal coin change via bottom-up DP table; returns table rows for UI visualization
user_interactions: Request DP algorithm by including "dp" in algorithms param

features.name: Backtracking Algorithm
features.description: Exhaustive coin change via backtracking; hard-capped at amount <= 60 to prevent server overload
user_interactions: Request backtracking algorithm by including "backtracking" in algorithms param
See error response if amount exceeds 60

features.name: Performance Analysis
features.description: Benchmarks all three algorithms across increasing amounts and generates PNG comparison charts
user_interactions: Submit coins and max_amount via POST form on analysis page
View four benchmark graphs (time, coins used, memory, greedy gap)
View summary table with complexity info and greedy optimality gap

known_limitations.issue: Backtracking is hard-capped at amount <= 60 due to O(2^n) complexity
known_limitations.impact: Backtracking results are unavailable for amounts > 60; returns error message instead

known_limitations.issue: Greedy optimality is not determined by the greedy module itself; it requires a silent DP run for verification
known_limitations.impact: Extra DP computation always occurs when greedy is selected without DP

known_limitations.issue: Analysis graphs are written to static/graphs/ on disk; no cleanup between runs
known_limitations.impact: Old graphs may persist if analysis parameters change between runs
